title: Preparing a floor with self-levelling compound
subtitle: Pour and spread compound to create a flat tiling or flooring base
excerpt: How to use self-levelling floor compound to flatten a concrete or plywood subfloor before tiling or fitting sheet flooring: priming the surface, mixing the compound, pouring, spreading, and allowing to cure.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Wall and Floor Tiling syllabus.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pourable cement or gypsum-based floor preparation product that flows into low spots and sets to a flat surface. Available in thicknesses from 1 mm to 60 mm depending on the product. Requires a primed substrate and a perimeter seal to prevent it running under skirting boards.

content.text: Self-levelling floor compound is the practical solution for a floor that has dips, undulations, or old adhesive ridges left from a previous floor covering, before tiling or fitting a smooth new floor surface. It is not a structural repair: if the floor has broken concrete or subsiding areas, those must be repaired first. The compound works on sound but unlevel surfaces, filling dips and providing a smooth base.
When to use it
Before laying ceramic or porcelain tiles on a concrete floor that dips more than 3 mm over a 2-metre span.
Before fitting sheet vinyl or LVT where an uneven surface would show through or cause the covering to crack.
To bury old adhesive residue or low-relief tile patterns.
What you need

SBR primer diluted as instructed; self-levelling compound (quantity calculated from area and depth); a clean bucket; a power drill with a paddle mixer; a gauging trowel for spreading; and a spiked roller to release air bubbles. Wear gloves: the compound is alkaline.
Step-by-step
Step 1, seal gaps.

The compound is pourable and will run under skirting boards and through any gap at the perimeter. Seal the junction between the floor and the skirting board with a bead of flexible sealant or tape. Fill any cracks wider than 2 mm with repair mortar and allow to set hard before pouring.
Step 2, prime the substrate.

Apply diluted SBR primer evenly across the floor with a brush or roller, working it into the surface. Allow to become tacky (not wet) before pouring the compound. On very porous or dusty concrete, a second primer coat gives better adhesion. Never pour compound onto an unprimed surface; the substrate will draw water from the compound too fast, causing it to crack as it sets.
Step 3, mix and pour.
Mix the compound in small batches (follow the manufacturer's water-to-powder ratio exactly) using the paddle mixer. Work quickly:
self-levelling compound

has a short pot life of 10 to 15 minutes. Pour from one corner and guide the flow with a gauging trowel. The compound self-levels to about 3 mm on a reasonably level floor; it will not move further on its own than the thickness allows.
Step 4, release air and cure.

Run a spiked roller across the fresh pour immediately after each section to release trapped air bubbles. Avoid foot traffic for at least 2 hours. The compound is typically ready for tiling or flooring after 24 hours, but check the manufacturer's data sheet for the specific product.
What it looks like when done right

A cured self-levelling floor is smooth, with a slight sheen and a hard surface that resists scratching with a fingernail. When checked with a long spirit level, variations should be within 3 mm over 2 metres. Any visible surface cracks (fine, not structural) indicate that the compound cured too fast, usually because the primer coat was too thin or the compound was mixed too dry.
Common mistakes
Skipping the primer.
The most common failure. Unprimed concrete draws water from the compound immediately on contact; the surface dries before it can flow and levels poorly.
Mixing too much at once.
Mix no more than you can pour in 10 minutes. Compound that has started to set cannot be thinned with extra water; adding water destroys the strength of the mix.
Opening doors and windows during curing.
Draughts cause uneven drying and surface cracking. Keep the room still and closed while the compound sets.
